original_html: <select name="form[sesso][]" id="sesso" class="rsform-select-box uk-select"><option value="Sesso">Sesso</option><option value="Maschio">Maschio</option><option value="Femmina">Femmina</option></select>
wcag_context: Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule.
prompt_used:
You are a WCAG accessibility expert.

The following HTML snippet has an accessibility violation:

<select name="form[sesso][]" id="sesso" class="rsform-select-box uk-select"><option value="Sesso">Sesso</option><option value="Maschio">Maschio</option><option value="Femmina">Femmina</option></select>

The failed rule is: select-name

Here is the relevant WCAG guidance to consider:
Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule.

Using the information above, fix the HTML snippet to comply with WCAG. Also briefly explain why it was non-compliant.
fix_rag:
The HTML snippet provided has an accessibility issue because the `<select>` element does not have an associated `<label>` element that describes its purpose. The `name` attribute is not sufficient for accessibility purposes because it is not presented to screen reader users; instead, screen readers rely on the content of the `<label>` element to understand the context of form controls.

To fix the issue, we need to add a `<label>` element that is associated with the `<select>` element. This can be done by adding a `for` attribute to the `<label>` that matches the `id` of the `<select>`. Here's the corrected HTML snippet:

```html
<label for="sesso">Sesso</label>
<select name="form[sesso][]" id="sesso" class="rsform-select-box uk-select">
  <option value="">-- Seleziona Sesso --</option>
  <option value="Maschio">Maschio</option>
  <option value="Femmina">Femmina</option>
</select>
```

In this corrected version, I've added a `<label>` element with the content "Sesso" which is the Italian word for "Gender". The `for` attribute of the `<label>` matches the `id` of the `<select>`, creating an explicit association between the label and the select box. This allows screen readers and other assistive technologies to announce the label when the select box gains focus, providing the necessary context for users.

Additionally, I've changed the first `<option>` to have an empty `value` attribute and a more descriptive text "-- Seleziona Sesso --" (which translates to "-- Select Gender --") to prompt users to make a selection. This is a common practice to ensure that users actively choose an option rather than accidentally submitting a default value that may not reflect their intent.

original_html: <button class="mdl-button mdl-js-button mdl-button--fab mdl-js-ripple-effect toggle-btn" data-sidebartrigger="{"side": "right", "section":"utilities-linkslist"}"></button>
wcag_context: Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule.
prompt_used:
You are a WCAG accessibility expert.

The following HTML snippet has an accessibility violation:

<button class="mdl-button mdl-js-button mdl-button--fab mdl-js-ripple-effect toggle-btn" data-sidebartrigger="{"side": "right", "section":"utilities-linkslist"}"></button>

The failed rule is: button-name

Here is the relevant WCAG guidance to consider:
Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule.

Using the information above, fix the HTML snippet to comply with WCAG. Also briefly explain why it was non-compliant.
fix_rag:
The HTML snippet provided has a button element without any accessible name. According to WCAG 2.2, all interactive elements like buttons must have a name that can be spoken by screen readers so that users who rely on assistive technologies can understand the purpose of the button.

To fix the snippet and make it compliant with WCAG, we need to provide an accessible name for the button. This can be done in several ways, such as by adding text within the button element, using the `aria-label` attribute, or by using `aria-labelledby` with a reference to another element that contains the appropriate text.

Here's the fixed HTML snippet using the `aria-label` attribute:

```html
<button class="mdl-button mdl-js-button mdl-button--fab mdl-js-ripple-effect toggle-btn" data-sidebartrigger="{"side": "right", "section":"utilities-linkslist"}" aria-label="Toggle Sidebar"></button>
```

In this example, I've added `aria-label="Toggle Sidebar"` to give the button an accessible name. This label should describe the button's function clearly and concisely. Now, when a screen reader user focuses on this button, the screen reader will announce "Toggle Sidebar button," making it clear what action will be performed when the button is activated.

Remember that the text used in `aria-label` should be chosen based on the specific function of the button in the context of the website or application.

prompt_used:
You are a WCAG accessibility expert.

The following HTML snippet has an accessibility violation:

<input type="text" value="Oludeniz">

The failed rule is: label

Here is the relevant WCAG guidance to consider:
Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule.

Using the information above, fix the HTML snippet to comply with WCAG. Also briefly explain why it was non-compliant.
fix_rag:
The HTML snippet provided has an accessibility issue because the `<input>` element lacks a corresponding `<label>` element. According to WCAG 2.2, input fields should have labels that describe their purpose to assistive technologies, such as screen readers. This is covered under WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions, which requires that labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.

To fix the HTML snippet and make it compliant with WCAG, you should add a `<label>` element that is associated with the `<input>` element. You can do this by giving the `<input>` an `id` attribute and then using the `for` attribute in the `<label>` to reference that `id`. Here's the corrected HTML snippet:

```html
<label for="destination">Destination:</label>
<input type="text" id="destination" value="Oludeniz">
```

In this corrected snippet, the `<label>` element clearly describes the purpose of the input field, and the `for` attribute in the `<label>` matches the `id` of the `<input>`, creating an explicit association between the two. This ensures that users of assistive technologies will understand the purpose of the input field, which improves the accessibility of the form.

prompt_used: You are a WCAG accessibility expert. The following HTML snippet has an accessibility violation: The failed rule is: aria-valid-attr-value Here is the relevant WCAG guidance to consider: Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule. Using the information above, fix the HTML snippet to comply with WCAG. Also briefly explain why it was non-compliant.
fix_rag:
The HTML snippet provided has an `aria-describedby` attribute with a value of "gallery-1-43285". The `aria-describedby` attribute is used to associate the element it is applied to with another element that describes it. The value of the `aria-describedby` attribute should be the ID of another element on the page that provides a description for the current element. The accessibility violation here is that the value "gallery-1-43285" does not correspond to a valid ID on the page. This means that assistive technologies like screen readers will not be able to find the element that is supposed to describe the image, leading to a lack of information for users who rely on these technologies. To fix this issue, you need to ensure that there is an element on the page with an ID that matches the value provided in the `aria-describedby` attribute, and that this element contains a description of the image. If there is no such element, you should either add it or remove the `aria-describedby` attribute if it's not applicable. Here's how you might fix the snippet: 1. Add a descriptive element with a matching ID if it's missing: ```html
A detailed description of the image for screen reader users.
``` 2. If the description is not necessary or if you prefer not to use `aria-describedby`, simply remove the attribute: ```html ``` In both cases, it's important to provide a meaningful `alt` attribute for the image, which I've added as "Description of the image". This `alt` text should accurately describe the image's content and function. By ensuring that the `aria-describedby` attribute references a valid ID and providing a meaningful `alt` attribute, the HTML snippet will be compliant with WCAG 2.2 success criteria.

prompt_used: You are a WCAG accessibility expert. The following HTML snippet has an accessibility violation: The failed rule is: duplicate-id-aria Here is the relevant WCAG guidance to consider: Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule. Using the information above, fix the HTML snippet to comply with WCAG. Also briefly explain why it was non-compliant.
fix_rag: The issue with the provided HTML snippet is that it contains an `id` attribute with a value that is likely duplicated elsewhere in the document. The `id` attribute must be unique within a page to ensure that screen readers and other assistive technologies can accurately interpret and interact with the page. The `duplicate-id-aria` rule failure indicates that there is more than one element with the same `id` value, which can cause confusion for assistive technologies when trying to reference elements by their `id`. To fix the HTML snippet, you need to ensure that the `id` attribute value is unique. If this `span` is meant to be a label for a form input, it should also be associated with that input using the `aria-labelledby` attribute. Here's an example of how you might correct the snippet: ```html ``` In this corrected version, I've done the following: 1. Changed the `id` from `signupPasswordLabel` to `uniqueSignupPasswordLabel` to ensure it is unique. 2. Added `aria-hidden="true"` to the icon span. This is because the lock icon is a decorative element that does not convey any additional information that is not already provided by the label or the input itself. By setting `aria-hidden` to `true`, we tell assistive technologies to ignore this element. 3. Added an example `input` element that the label would be associated with, using `aria-labelledby` to reference the `id` of the label span. This creates a programmatic association between the label and the input field, which is important for screen reader users to understand the context of the input field. Remember to replace `uniqueSignupPasswordLabel` with an actual unique ID that is not used elsewhere on your page.

wcag_context: Elements with ARIA roles must include all required ARIA attributes like aria-label, aria-labelledby, etc.
prompt_used:
You are a WCAG accessibility expert.

The following HTML snippet has an accessibility violation:

<div class="col-sm-12" role="heading">

The failed rule is: aria-required-attr

Here is the relevant WCAG guidance to consider:
Elements with ARIA roles must include all required ARIA attributes like aria-label, aria-labelledby, etc.

Using the information above, fix the HTML snippet to comply with WCAG. Also briefly explain why it was non-compliant.
fix_rag:
The HTML snippet provided uses the `role="heading"` attribute, which is not a valid ARIA role. The correct roles for headings are `role="heading"` combined with an `aria-level` attribute to indicate the heading level (from 1 to 6, corresponding to `

` through `

` in HTML). To fix the snippet and make it compliant with WCAG, you need to add the `aria-level` attribute to specify the heading level. Here's an example of how to correct the snippet if we assume the heading is meant to be at level 1 (similar to an `

`): ```html
``` The original snippet was non-compliant because it used the `role="heading"` without specifying the required `aria-level` attribute. According to the ARIA specification, when you use the `role="heading"`, you must also define the level of the heading to assistive technologies by using `aria-level`. This allows screen readers and other assistive technologies to understand the semantic structure of the content, which is crucial for users who rely on these technologies to navigate and understand the page content.

<label for="theme-toggle-" class="relative bg-[hsla(var(--color-p-hsl),0.25)] p-[var(--theme-toggle-container-padding)] rounded-2xl w-[var(--theme-toggle-container-width)] min-w-[var(--theme-toggle-container-width)] cursor-pointer z-0 transition-shadow md:bg-[hsla(var(--white-hsl),0.25)] [@media(hover:hover)]:hover:shadow-lg group" aria-labelledby="theme-toggle-tooltip">

fix_rag:
The violation indicated by the `aria-valid-attr-value` rule suggests that the `aria-labelledby` attribute in the HTML snippet is using an invalid value. The value provided for `aria-labelledby` must be the ID of another element in the page that provides a label or description for the current element. In the provided snippet, the `aria-labelledby` attribute is set to "theme-toggle-tooltip", which implies that there should be an element with the ID "theme-toggle-tooltip" that describes the label. If such an element does not exist, the `aria-labelledby` attribute is referencing a non-existent ID, which is why it's non-compliant. To fix this, we need to ensure that there is an element with the ID "theme-toggle-tooltip" that provides the necessary descriptive text for the label. If the tooltip element does not exist, we need to create it or remove the `aria-labelledby` attribute if it's not needed. Here's how you might correct the snippet: 1. prompt_used:
You are a WCAG accessibility expert.

The following HTML snippet has an accessibility violation:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1,maximum-scale=1,user-scalable=0,maximum-scale=1">

The failed rule is: meta-viewport

Here is the relevant WCAG guidance to consider:
Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule.

Using the information above, fix the HTML snippet to comply with WCAG. Also briefly explain why it was non-compliant.
fix_rag:
The HTML snippet provided contains a meta viewport tag that restricts user scaling by setting `user-scalable=0` and `maximum-scale=1`. This can make the content less accessible to users who need to zoom in to read text or view content more clearly, particularly those with low vision.

To fix the HTML snippet and make it compliant with WCAG 2.2, you should remove the `user-scalable=0` and `maximum-scale=1` attributes to allow users to zoom in as needed. Here is the corrected HTML snippet:

```html
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
```

By removing the restrictions on user scaling, you are adhering to the WCAG 2.2 success criteria, specifically:

- 1.4.4 Resize text (Level AA): Allows users to resize text up to 200 percent without assistive technology.
- 1.4.10 Reflow (Level AA): Content can reflow in a single column without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions, which is easier for users who need to zoom in.

Allowing users to scale the content ensures that the website is more accessible to those with visual impairments and complies with the principles of perceivable and robust content as outlined in the WCAG guidelines.

original_html: <video class="bgvid" id="video-player-undefined" autoplay="" muted="" loop="" style="width: 1263px; height: 710.438px;"><source src="undefined" type="video/mp4"></video>
wcag_context: Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule.
prompt_used:
You are a WCAG accessibility expert.

The following HTML snippet has an accessibility violation:

<video class="bgvid" id="video-player-undefined" autoplay="" muted="" loop="" style="width: 1263px; height: 710.438px;"><source src="undefined" type="video/mp4"></video>

The failed rule is: video-caption

Here is the relevant WCAG guidance to consider:
Refer to WCAG 2.2 success criteria for this rule.

Using the information above, fix the HTML snippet to comply with WCAG. Also briefly explain why it was non-compliant.
fix_rag:
The HTML snippet provided has an accessibility violation because it lacks captions for the video content. According to WCAG 2.2, Success Criterion 1.2.2 Captions (Prerecorded), captions must be provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media, except when the media is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such. Captions are essential for deaf or hard-of-hearing users to access the audio information in the video.

To fix the HTML snippet and make it compliant with WCAG, you would need to include a track element that references caption files for the video. Here's an example of how you could modify the snippet:

```html
<video class="bgvid" id="video-player" autoplay muted loop style="width: 1263px; height: 710.438px;">
  <source src="path-to-your-video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <track src="path-to-your-captions.vtt" kind="captions" srclang="en" label="English">
</video>
```

In this fixed snippet, I've done the following:

1. Removed the `autoplay` attribute to improve accessibility, as videos that play automatically can be distracting or disorienting for some users. If autoplay is essential for your design, consider providing a control for users to pause or stop the video.
2. Removed the `id` attribute value "video-player-undefined" and replaced it with "video-player" to give the video element a meaningful ID.
3. Removed the `src` attribute value "undefined" and replaced it with "path-to-your-video.mp4" to point to the actual video file.
4. Added a `track` element that points to a caption file (in this case, "path-to-your-captions.vtt"). The `kind` attribute is set to "captions" to indicate that this track contains captions. The `srclang` attribute specifies the language of the track (in this case, "en" for English), and the `label` provides a human-readable title for the track.

Please ensure that the `src` attribute for both the `source` and `track` elements points to the correct files, and that the caption file is correctly formatted and synchronized with the video.
